{
  "content": {
    "tool_use_id": "toolu_01P3PU3voc1KXkV4CEfp7HqU",
    "type": "tool_result",
    "content": "     1\u2192/**\n     2\u2192 * Character.js \u2014 tile-based character with A* pathfinding and state machine.\n     3\u2192 * Pure JS, no Phaser dependency. Sprite/label references are set externally.\n     4\u2192 */\n     5\u2192\n     6\u2192const TILE_SIZE = 48;\n     7\u2192\n     8\u2192// \u2500\u2500\u2500 A* pathfinding \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n     9\u2192\n    10\u2192function heuristic(ax, ay, bx, by) {\n    11\u2192  return Math.abs(ax - bx) + Math.abs(ay - by);\n    12\u2192}\n    13\u2192\n    14\u2192/**\n    15\u2192 * Find a path from (startX, startY) to (goalX, goalY) on a walkable grid.\n    16\u2192 * @param {boolean[][]} walkable  - walkable[row][col]\n    17\u2192 * @param {number} cols\n    18\u2192 * @param {number} rows\n    19\u2192 * @returns {Array<{tx,ty}>|null}  - list of steps NOT including start; null if no path\n    20\u2192 */\n    21\u2192function findPath(walkable, cols, rows, startX, startY, goalX, goalY) {\n    22\u2192  if (startX === goalX && startY === goalY) return [];\n    23\u2192  if (!walkable[startY]?.[startX] || !walkable[goalY]?.[goalX]) return null;\n    24\u2192\n    25\u2192  const key = (x, y) => x * 1000 + y;\n    26\u2192\n    27\u2192  const open = new Map();\n    28\u2192  const closed = new Set();\n    29\u2192\n    30\u2192  const startNode = { x: startX, y: startY, g: 0, h: heuristic(startX, startY, goalX, goalY), parent: null };\n    31\u2192  open.set(key(startX, startY), startNode);\n    32\u2192\n    33\u2192  while (open.size > 0) {\n    34\u2192    // Pick node with lowest f = g + h\n    35\u2192    let current = null;\n    36\u2192    for (const node of open.values()) {\n    37\u2192      if (!current || node.g + node.h < current.g + current.h) current = node;\n    38\u2192    }\n    39\u2192\n    40\u2192    if (current.x === goalX && current.y === goalY) {\n    41\u2192      // Reconstruct path\n    42\u2192      const path = [];\n    43\u2192      let node = current;\n    44\u2192      while (node.parent) {\n    45\u2192        path.unshift({ tx: node.x, ty: node.y });\n    46\u2192        node = node.parent;\n    47\u2192      }\n    48\u2192      return path;\n    49\u2192    }\n    50\u2192\n    51\u2192    open.delete(key(current.x, current.y));\n    52\u2192    closed.add(key(current.x, current.y));\n    53\u2192\n    54\u2192    const neighbors = [\n    55\u2192      { x: current.x + 1, y: current.y },\n    56\u2192      { x: current.x - 1, y: current.y },\n    57\u2192      { x: current.x,     y: current.y + 1 },\n    58\u2192      { x: current.x,     y: current.y - 1 },\n    59\u2192    ];\n    60\u2192\n    61\u2192    for (const n of neighbors) {\n    62\u2192      if (n.x < 0 || n.x >= cols || n.y < 0 || n.y >= rows) continue;\n    63\u2192      if (!walkable[n.y]?.[n.x]) continue;\n    64\u2192      if (closed.has(key(n.x, n.y))) continue;\n    65\u2192\n    66\u2192      const g = current.g + 1;\n    67\u2192      const existing = open.get(key(n.x, n.y));\n    68\u2192      if (existing) {\n    69\u2192        if (g < existing.g) {\n    70\u2192          existing.g = g;\n    71\u2192          existing.parent = current;\n    72\u2192        }\n    73\u2192      } else {\n    74\u2192        open.set(key(n.x, n.y), {\n    75\u2192          x: n.x, y: n.y,\n    76\u2192          g, h: heuristic(n.x, n.y, goalX, goalY),\n    77\u2192          parent: current,\n    78\u2192        });\n    79\u2192      }\n    80\u2192    }\n    81\u2192  }\n    82\u2192\n    83\u2192  return null; // no path\n    84\u2192}\n    85\u2192\n    86\u2192// \u2500\u2500\u2500 Character \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n    87\u2192\n    88\u2192export class Character {\n    89\u2192  /**\n    90\u2192   * @param {object} config\n    91\u2192   * @param {string}   config.name        - sprite key prefix (e.g. 'Amelia')\n    92\u2192   * @param {string}   config.label       - display name (e.g. 'Maple')\n    93\u2192   * @param {number}   config.tx          - starting tile X\n    94\u2192   * @param {number}   config.ty          - starting tile Y\n    95\u2192   * @param {boolean[][]} config.walkable - walkable grid [row][col]\n    96\u2192   * @param {number}   config.gridCols\n    97\u2192   * @param {number}   config.gridRows\n    98\u2192   * @param {object}   [config.sprite]    - Phaser sprite (set after construction)\n    99\u2192   * @param {object}   [config.nameLabel] - Phaser Text object\n   100\u2192   * @param {number}   [config.moveSpeed] - tiles per second (default 2)\n   101\u2192   */\n   102\u2192  constructor(config) {\n   103\u2192    this.name      = config.name;\n   104\u2192    this.label     = config.label;\n   105\u2192    this.sprite    = config.sprite    || null;\n   106\u2192    this.nameLabel = config.nameLabel || null;\n   107\u2192\n   108\u2192    this.tx     = config.tx;\n   109\u2192    this.ty     = config.ty;\n   110\u2192    this.prevTx = config.tx;\n   111\u2192    this.prevTy = config.ty;\n   112\u2192\n   113\u2192    this.path        = [];\n   114\u2192    this.moveProgress = 0;\n   115\u2192    this.moveSpeed   = config.moveSpeed || 2; // tiles/sec\n   116\u2192\n   117\u2192    // State: 'idle' | 'walking' | 'interacting' | 'talking'\n   118\u2192    this.action = 'idle';\n   119\u2192    this.facing = 'down';\n   120\u2192\n   121\u2192    // Wander: timer counts DOWN; engine starts wander when <= 0\n   122\u2192    this.wanderTimer = 3000 + Math.random() * 5000; // 3\u20138 s\n   123\u2192\n   124\u2192    // Interaction\n   125\u2192    this.interactTimer    = 0;\n   126\u2192    this.interactTimerMax = 0; // initial duration; used by UI for progress bar\n   127\u2192    this.interactTarget   = null;\n   128\u2192\n   129\u2192    // Personality stats (0\u2013100)\n   130\u2192    this.mood   = 100;\n   131\u2192    this.energy = 100;\n   132\u2192\n   133\u2192    // Optional dialogue driven by AI\n   134\u2192    this.currentDialogue = null;\n   135\u2192    this.dialogueTimer   = 0;\n   136\u2192\n   137\u2192    // Preset dialogue lines (used for random speech bubbles)\n   138\u2192    this.dialogue     = config.dialogue || [];\n   139\u2192    // Counts down; when it hits 0 Engine sets pendingSpeech\n   140\u2192    this.speechTimer  = 20000 + Math.random() * 20000; // 20\u201340 s\n   141\u2192    // Scene reads this each frame and shows a bubble, then clears it\n   142\u2192    this.pendingSpeech = null;\n   143\u2192\n   144\u2192    this.walkable  = config.walkable;\n   145\u2192    this.gridCols  = config.gridCols;\n   146\u2192    this.gridRows  = config.gridRows;\n   147\u2192\n   148\u2192    // Object interaction targeting \u2014 set by engine, cleared on arrival\n   149\u2192    this.targetObjectId = null;\n   150\u2192    this.pendingAction  = null;\n   151\u2192\n   152\u2192    // Fallback/re-routing: counts down; engine updates talk route when <= 0\n   153\u2192    this._talkRetargetTimer = 0;\n   154\u2192\n   155\u2192    // Deadlock escape: counts consecutive failed wander attempts; resets on success\n   156\u2192    this._failedMoveCount = 0;\n   157\u2192\n   158\u2192    // Talk targeting \u2014 set by AIEngine when walking to meet another character\n   159\u2192    this.pendingTalkTarget   = null;  // Character ref awaiting arrival\n   160\u2192    this.pendingTalkDialogue = null;  // What A will say when conversation starts\n   161\u2192    this.pendingTalkResponse = null;  // What B will say when conversation starts\n   162\u2192\n   163\u2192    // Active conversation state\n   164\u2192    this.talkTimer    = 0;\n   165\u2192    this.talkTimerMax = 0;\n   166\u2192    this.talkTarget   = null;  // Character ref of current conversation partner\n   167\u2192  }\n   168\u2192\n   169\u2192  // \u2500\u2500 Computed pixel position (Phaser setOrigin(0.5, 1) \u2014 feet at py) \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n   170\u2192\n   171\u2192  get px() {\n   172\u2192    if (this.path.length === 0) {\n   173\u2192      return this.tx * TILE_SIZE + TILE_SIZE / 2;\n   174\u2192    }\n   175\u2192    const next = this.path[0];\n   176\u2192    return (this.prevTx + (next.tx - this.prevTx) * this.moveProgress) * TILE_SIZE + TILE_SIZE / 2;\n   177\u2192  }\n   178\u2192\n   179\u2192  get py() {\n   180\u2192    if (this.path.length === 0) {\n   181\u2192      return (this.ty + 1) * TILE_SIZE;\n   182\u2192    }\n   183\u2192    const next = this.path[0];\n   184\u2192    return (this.prevTy + (next.ty - this.prevTy) * this.moveProgress + 1) * TILE_SIZE;\n   185\u2192  }\n   186\u2192\n   187\u2192  // \u2500\u2500 Movement \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n   188\u2192\n   189\u2192  /**\n   190\u2192   * @param {number} goalTx\n   191\u2192   * @param {number} goalTy\n   192\u2192   * @param {boolean[][]} [walkable] - optional dynamic walkable grid; defaults to this.walkable\n   193\u2192   */\n   194\u2192  moveTo(goalTx, goalTy, walkable = this.walkable) {\n   195\u2192    const sx = Math.round(this.tx);\n   196\u2192    const sy = Math.round(this.ty);\n   197\u2192    const path = findPath(walkable, this.gridCols, this.gridRows, sx, sy, goalTx, goalTy);\n   198\u2192\n   199\u2192    if (path && path.length > 0) {\n   200\u2192      this.path         = path;\n   201\u2192      this.prevTx       = sx;\n   202\u2192      this.prevTy       = sy;\n   203\u2192      this.moveProgress = 0;\n   204\u2192      this.action       = 'walking';\n   205\u2192\n   206\u2192      // Reset deadlock counter on successful path\n   207\u2192      this._failedMoveCount = 0;\n   208\u2192\n   209\u2192      // Set initial facing from first step\n   210\u2192      this._updateFacingFromNextTile();\n   211\u2192      this._playWalkAnim();\n   212\u2192      return true;\n   213\u2192    }\n   214\u2192    return false;\n   215\u2192  }\n   216\u2192\n   217\u2192  /** Convenience wrapper \u2014 start interaction with a named object. */\n   218\u2192  startInteraction(objectId, durationMs, animType) {\n   219\u2192    this.startInteract(objectId, durationMs, animType);\n   220\u2192  }\n   221\u2192\n   222\u2192  startInteract(target, durationMs, animType) {\n   223\u2192    this.action           = 'interacting';\n   224\u2192    this.interactTarget   = target;\n   225\u2192    this.interactTimer    = durationMs;\n   226\u2192    this.interactTimerMax = durationMs;\n   227\u2192    if (this.sprite) {\n   228\u2192      const interactKey = animType ? `${this.name}_interact_${animType}` : null;\n   229\u2192      const key = (interactKey && this.sprite.anims.exists(interactKey))\n   230\u2192        ? interactKey\n   231\u2192        : `${this.name}_idle_anim`;\n   232\u2192      this.sprite.play(key, true);\n   233\u2192    }\n   234\u2192  }\n   235\u2192\n   236\u2192  /** Enter a shared talking state with `partner` for `durationMs` milliseconds. */\n   237\u2192  startTalk(partner, durationMs) {\n   238\u2192    // Stop any current movement\n   239\u2192    this.path        = [];\n   240\u2192    this.moveProgress = 0;\n   241\u2192\n   242\u2192    this.action       = 'talking';\n   243\u2192    this.talkTarget   = partner;\n   244\u2192    this.talkTimer    = durationMs;\n   245\u2192    this.talkTimerMax = durationMs;\n   246\u2192\n   247\u2192    // Clear any pending object interaction targeting\n   248\u2192    this.targetObjectId      = null;\n   249\u2192    this.pendingAction       = null;\n   250\u2192    // Clear any pending talk targeting (e.g. B was also walking to someone)\n   251\u2192    this.pendingTalkTarget   = null;\n   252\u2192    this.pendingTalkDialogue = null;\n   253\u2192    this.pendingTalkResponse = null;\n   254\u2192\n   255\u2192    if (this.sprite) {\n   256\u2192      this.sprite.play(`${this.name}_idle_anim`, true);\n   257\u2192    }\n   258\u2192  }\n   259\u2192\n   260\u2192  // \u2500\u2500 Per-frame update \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n   261\u2192\n   262\u2192  update(deltaMs) {\n   263\u2192    if (this.action === 'walking' && this.path.length > 0) {\n   264\u2192      const tileDurationMs = 1000 / this.moveSpeed;\n   265\u2192      this.moveProgress += deltaMs / tileDurationMs;\n   266\u2192\n   267\u2192      // Advance tiles\n   268\u2192      while (this.moveProgress >= 1 && this.path.length > 0) {\n   269\u2192        this.moveProgress -= 1;\n   270\u2192        const arrived = this.path.shift();\n   271\u2192        this.prevTx = arrived.tx;\n   272\u2192        this.prevTy = arrived.ty;\n   273\u2192        this.tx     = arrived.tx;\n   274\u2192        this.ty     = arrived.ty;\n   275\u2192      }\n   276\u2192\n   277\u2192      // Update facing after tile advance so animation matches the current movement direction\n   278\u2192      this._updateFacingFromNextTile();\n   279\u2192\n   280\u2192      if (this.path.length === 0) {\n   281\u2192        this.moveProgress = 0;\n   282\u2192        this.action       = 'idle';\n   283\u2192        this.wanderTimer  = 3000 + Math.random() * 5000;\n   284\u2192        this._playAnim('idle');\n   285\u2192      } else {\n   286\u2192        this._playWalkAnim();\n   287\u2192      }\n   288\u2192    } else if (this.action === 'idle') {\n   289\u2192      this.wanderTimer -= deltaMs;\n   290\u2192      // Ensure idle animation is always playing in idle state (handles external action resets)\n   291\u2192      this._playAnim('idle');\n   292\u2192    } else if (this.action === 'interacting') {\n   293\u2192      this.interactTimer -= deltaMs;\n   294\u2192      if (this.interactTimer <= 0) {\n   295\u2192        this.action        = 'idle';\n   296\u2192        this.interactTarget = null;\n   297\u2192        this.wanderTimer   = 3000 + Math.random() * 5000;\n   298\u2192        this._playAnim('idle');\n   299\u2192      }\n   300\u2192    } else if (this.action === 'talking') {\n   301\u2192      this.talkTimer -= deltaMs;\n   302\u2192      if (this.talkTimer <= 0) {\n   303\u2192        this.action      = 'idle';\n   304\u2192        this.talkTarget  = null;\n   305\u2192        this.wanderTimer = 3000 + Math.random() * 5000;\n   306\u2192        this._playAnim('idle');\n   307\u2192      }\n   308\u2192    }\n   309\u2192\n   310\u2192    // Tick dialogue\n   311\u2192    if (this.dialogueTimer > 0) {\n   312\u2192      this.dialogueTimer -= deltaMs;\n   313\u2192      if (this.dialogueTimer <= 0) this.currentDialogue = null;\n   314\u2192    }\n   315\u2192\n   316\u2192    // Sync Phaser sprite\n   317\u2192    if (this.sprite) {\n   318\u2192      this.sprite.x = this.px;\n   319\u2192      this.sprite.y = this.py;\n   320\u2192    }\n   321\u2192\n   322\u2192    if (this.nameLabel && this.sprite) {\n   323\u2192      this.nameLabel.setPosition(this.sprite.x, this.sprite.y - 100);\n   324\u2192    }\n   325\u2192  }\n   326\u2192\n   327\u2192  // \u2500\u2500 Perception \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n   328\u2192\n   329\u2192  /** Returns objects within `radius` tiles. */\n   330\u2192  getPerceivedObjects(objects, radius = 4) {\n   331\u2192    return objects.filter(obj => {\n   332\u2192      const dx = obj.tx - this.tx;\n   333\u2192      const dy = obj.ty - this.ty;\n   334\u2192      return Math.sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy) <= radius;\n   335\u2192    });\n   336\u2192  }\n   337\u2192\n   338\u2192  // \u2500\u2500 Animation helpers \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n   339\u2192\n   340\u2192  _updateFacingFromNextTile() {\n   341\u2192    if (this.path.length === 0) return;\n   342\u2192    const next = this.path[0];\n   343\u2192    const dx   = next.tx - this.tx;\n   344\u2192    const dy   = next.ty - this.ty;\n   345\u2192    if (Math.abs(dx) >= Math.abs(dy)) {\n   346\u2192      this.facing = dx >= 0 ? 'right' : 'left';\n   347\u2192    } else {\n   348\u2192      this.facing = dy > 0 ? 'down' : 'up';\n   349\u2192    }\n   350\u2192  }\n   351\u2192\n   352\u2192  _playAnim(type) {\n   353\u2192    if (!this.sprite) return;\n   354\u2192    const key = type === 'idle'\n   355\u2192      ? `${this.name}_idle_anim`\n   356\u2192      : `${this.name}_walk_${this.facing}`;\n   357\u2192    if (this.sprite.anims.currentAnim?.key !== key) {\n   358\u2192      this.sprite.play(key, true);\n   359\u2192    }\n   360\u2192    this.sprite.flipX = (type === 'walk' && this.facing === 'left');\n   361\u2192  }\n   362\u2192\n   363\u2192  _playWalkAnim() {\n   364\u2192    this._playAnim('walk');\n   365\u2192  }\n   366\u2192}\n   367\u2192\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"
  }

Let me understand what the existing tests test vs. what I should test: Existing tests (based on wrong API): 1. `_moveCharWithCollision` → doesn't exist 2. `_findFreeInteractTile` → doesn't exist 3. `_retryTimer`, `_retryCount`, `_pendingTarget` → don't exist 4. `manualTick` → doesn't exist 5. `aiMode` → doesn't exist 6. `snapshot()` → doesn't exist What I need to test (from the task instructions and actual implementation): 1. Characters don't share tiles during sim - use `engine.update(deltaMs)` loop 2. `_buildDynamicWalkable` marks other chars as blocked, doesn't modify static grid 3. `moveTo` uses dynamic walkable grid 4. Deadlock escape after 3 failures - use `engine._doWander(char)` calls 5. Live sim stability - use `engine.update(deltaMs)` loop Now I need to figure out how to access the engine from the browser and start the dev server.

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #78
There's no start-overlay in this project. Good, so the existing test's `injectEngineExposure` that patches the page and the `startEngine` that looks for `#start-overlay` won't be needed. The engine is already exposed in the game. Also, there's no `timeScale` property in the engine. The engine's `update()` receives `deltaMs` naturally from Phaser. From a test perspective, we can directly call `engine.update(deltaMs)` from the browser to simulate ticks. Let me also check what other tests do to understand how to handle the Phaser initialization. The other test specs might give me clues.Let me look at a working test like character-facing.spec.js or walk-animation.spec.js which also run against a local server.

USERMESSAGE #80
✓ TOOL RESULT
SUCCESS 
     1→'use strict';
     2→/**
     3→ * REGRESSION — FRG-244
     4→ * Characters face toward objects when interacting (and toward each other when talking).
     5→ *
     6→ * Tests run against the local dev server (http://localhost:5173) because this
     7→ * feature lives in the feature/frg-244 branch, not yet deployed to Vercel.
     8→ *
     9→ * The page is served as a static file and does NOT expose window._gameEngine
    10→ * by default, so we intercept index.html via page.route() and inject the
    11→ * exposure before the script executes.
    12→ *
    13→ * Test layout:
    14→ *   1. Object-facing regression — character faces the object it interacts with
    15→ *   2. Talk facing (direct) — facing logic is correct for character-to-character
    16→ *   3. Talk arrival facing — when a character walks to another and arrives, both face each other
    17→ */
    18→const { test, expect } = require('@playwright/test');
    19→
    20→const LOCAL_URL = 'http://localhost:5173';
    21→
    22→// ── helpers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    23→
    24→/**
    25→ * Intercept index.html served at / and inject window._gameEngine = engine
    26→ * so tests can call page.evaluate(() => window._gameEngine.*).
    27→ */
    28→async function injectEngineExposure(page) {
    29→  await page.route('**/', async (route) => {
    30→    const response = await route.fetch();
    31→    let body = await response.text();
    32→    // Inject just before "// ── Initial state ──" at the bottom of the module script
    33→    body = body.replace(
    34→      '// ── Initial state ──',
    35→      '// ── Initial state (QA injection) ──
window._gameEngine = engine;'
    36→    );
    37→    await route.fulfill({ response, body });
    38→  });
    39→}
    40→
    41→/**
    42→ * Start the engine — click the overlay (click-to-start) then boost speed.
    43→ */
    44→async function startEngine(page, timeScale = 20) {
    45→  // Click the overlay to start the sim
    46→  const overlay = page.locator('#start-overlay');
    47→  if (await overlay.isVisible()) {
    48→    await overlay.click();
    49→  }
    50→
    51→  // Boost speed for faster interactions
    52→  await page.evaluate((scale) => {
    53→    if (window._gameEngine) window._gameEngine.timeScale = scale;
    54→  }, timeScale);
    55→}
    56→
    57→/**
    58→ * Poll engine snapshot until predicate returns truthy.
    59→ */
    60→async function pollSnapshot(page, predicate, timeoutMs = 45_000, intervalMs = 300) {

USERMESSAGE #82
✓ TOOL RESULT
SUCCESS 
     1→'use strict';
     2→/**
     3→ * REGRESSION — FRG-204
     4→ * Walk animations stutter/freeze mid-movement due to timer accumulation issues.
     5→ *
     6→ * Root cause: walk frame timer was not using a while-loop, so large deltaMs values
     7→ * (e.g. when tab was backgrounded or frame rate dropped) only advanced walkFrame
     8→ * once per update() call, leaving walkTimer > walkFrameMs and causing the animation
     9→ * to stutter or freeze.
    10→ *
    11→ * Fix: advance walkFrame in a while-loop, consuming all accumulated intervals so
    12→ * walkTimer is always < walkFrameMs after each update().
    13→ *
    14→ * Tests run against the local dev server (http://localhost:5174) which serves
    15→ * the feature/frg-204-fix-walk-animation branch.
    16→ *
    17→ * The page does NOT expose window._gameEngine by default, so we intercept
    18→ * index.html via page.route() and inject the exposure before the script executes.
    19→ *
    20→ * Test layout:
    21→ *   1. moveTo resets walk state — walkFrame=0 and walkTimer=0 on new movement
    22→ *   2. Large deltaMs — update(1000) advances all 5 frames, walkTimer holds only remainder
    23→ *   3. moveTo resets dirty walk state — pre-set walkFrame/walkTimer are cleared on moveTo
    24→ *   4. Live sim regression — no character gets stuck on same walkFrame while walking for 30s
    25→ */
    26→const { test, expect } = require('@playwright/test');
    27→
    28→const LOCAL_URL = 'http://localhost:5174';
    29→
    30→// walkFrameMs is 200ms per frame (4-frame cycle)
    31→const WALK_FRAME_MS = 200;
    32→const WALK_FRAME_COUNT = 4;
    33→
    34→// ── helpers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    35→
    36→/**
    37→ * Intercept index.html served at / and inject window._gameEngine = engine
    38→ * so tests can call page.evaluate(() => window._gameEngine.*).
    39→ */
    40→async function injectEngineExposure(page) {
    41→  await page.route('**/', async (route) => {
    42→    const response = await route.fetch();
    43→    let body = await response.text();
    44→    body = body.replace(
    45→      '// ── Initial state ──',
    46→      '// ── Initial state (QA injection) ──
window._gameEngine = engine;'
    47→    );
    48→    await route.fulfill({ response, body });
    49→  });
    50→}
    51→
    52→/**
    53→ * Click start overlay (if present) and set timeScale.
    54→ */
    55→async function startEngine(page, timeScale = 1) {
    56→  const overlay = page.locator('#start-overlay');
    57→  if (await overlay.isVisible()) {
    58→    await overlay.click();
    59→  }
    60→  if (timeScale !== 1) {

injection) \u2500\u2500\\nwindow._gameEngine = engine;'\n    47\u2192    );\n    48\u2192    await route.fulfill({ response, body });\n    49\u2192  });\n    50\u2192}\n    51\u2192\n    52\u2192/**\n    53\u2192 * Click start overlay (if present) and set timeScale.\n    54\u2192 */\n    55\u2192async function startEngine(page, timeScale = 1) {\n    56\u2192  const overlay = page.locator('#start-overlay');\n    57\u2192  if (await overlay.isVisible()) {\n    58\u2192    await overlay.click();\n    59\u2192  }\n    60\u2192  if (timeScale !== 1) ASSISTANTMESSAGE #83
I see a pattern: 1. The older tests (FRG-244, FRG-204) use an injection approach where they replace `// ── Initial state ──` in the HTML to expose the engine. They also try to set `timeScale`. 2. But in the current source code (FRG-245 branch), the engine is already exposed via `window._gameEngine = this.engine;` directly in scene.js. Since the FRG-245 branch already exposes `window._gameEngine`, the injection is NOT needed for these tests. But it's harmless to keep it. However, the key problems with the existing test are: 1. Several methods and properties don't exist in the actual codebase: `_moveCharWithCollision`, `_findFreeInteractTile`, `_retryTimer`, `_retryCount`, `_pendingTarget`, `manualTick`, `aiMode`, `snapshot`, and the engine uses `getById()` instead of `get()` for object retrieval. 2. The test references `cafe_espresso` as an object ID, but that doesn't exist—the actual objects in the scene are stove, fridge, tea_set, kotatsu, tv, bookshelf, desk, bed, easel, and bonsai. I need to map the test's expectations to what's actually available in the API. Looking at the source code, I can confirm that `Engine._buildDynamicWalkable()`, `Engine._findAdjacentWalkable()`, and `Character.moveTo()` all exist as specified. The character's `_failedMoveCount` property is there, and the deadlock escape logic after 3 failed wanders is implemented in `_doWander()`. The wander retry backoff with 500-1000ms delays is also present, along with the methods `_doWander`, `_handleTalkArrival`, `_updateTalkRoute`, and `_fallbackToAlternative`. However, the test is using several methods and properties that don't actually exist in the engine. I need to map these to what's actually available: `engine.manualTick()` should be `engine.update(deltaMs)`, `engine.aiMode` doesn't exist—AI is controlled by setting `engine.aiEngine` to null or a valid instance, `engine.snapshot()` requires direct property access instead, `engine.objectEngine.get()` should be `engine.objectEngine.getById()`, `cafe_espresso` isn't defined in the object definitions so I'll need to use an actual object like stove or tea_set, and `_moveCharWithCollision` doesn't exist—the test should use `char.moveTo()` instead. There are also some helper methods the test references that aren't in the engine: `_findFreeInteractTile` should be replaced with `_findAdjacentWalkable` plus manual occupation checking, and the retry-related properties like `_retryTimer`, `_retryCount`, and `_pendingTarget` don't exist—I should use `char.wanderTimer` and `char._failedMoveCount` instead. Now I need to check the scene.js file to find the actual grid dimensions.

The test references `cafe_espresso` as an object ID, but that doesn't exist\u2014the actual objects in the scene are stove, fridge, tea_set, kotatsu, tv, bookshelf, desk, bed, easel, and bonsai.\n\nI need to map the test's expectations to what's actually available in the API.\n\nLooking at the source code, I can confirm that `Engine._buildDynamicWalkable()`, `Engine._findAdjacentWalkable()`, and `Character.moveTo()` all exist as specified. I need to map these to what's actually available: `engine.manualTick()` should be `engine.update(deltaMs)`, `engine.aiMode` doesn't exist\u2014AI is controlled by setting `engine.aiEngine` to null or a valid instance, `engine.snapshot()` requires direct property access instead, `engine.objectEngine.get()` should be `engine.objectEngine.getById()`, `cafe_espresso` isn't defined in the object definitions so I'll need to use an actual object like stove or tea_set, and `_moveCharWithCollision` doesn't exist\u2014the test should use `char.moveTo()` instead.\n\nThere are also some helper methods the test references that aren't in the engine: `_findFreeInteractTile` should be replaced with `_findAdjacentWalkable` plus manual occupation checking, and the retry-related properties like `_retryTimer`, `_retryCount`, and `_pendingTarget` don't exist\u2014I should use `char.wanderTimer` and `char._failedMoveCount` instead.\n\nNow I need to check the scene.js file to find the actual grid dimensions.",

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #91
`ObjectInstance` has both `position: { tx, ty }` AND `tx` and `ty` directly. So `obj.tx` and `obj.ty` work.Now let me understand the walkable grid. In scene.js, it's built from tilemap data. For testing purposes, I need to understand which tiles are walkable. Let me check how the tests can access the walkable grid, and what the actual walkable tiles look like. Looking at the engine's `_getWalkableTiles()` method: ```js _getWalkableTiles() { if (this._walkableTiles) return this._walkableTiles; ``` Building the walkable tiles... So `engine.walkable[row][col]` is the walkable grid. I can access it in the browser. The grid is 19 columns by 13 rows, and I need to figure out where to place test objects on valid walkable tiles. For the test setup, I can either check `engine.walkable` directly or call `_getWalkableTiles()` to get the list of valid positions. Since the engine's update loop is already running in real-time through Phaser, I can simulate time passing by calling `engine.update()` with larger delta values multiple times rather than waiting in real time. This lets me speed up the simulation without needing a timeScale property.

USERMESSAGE #93
✓ TOOL RESULT
SUCCESS 
     1→'use strict';
     2→/**
     3→ * REGRESSION — FRG-243
     4→ * Characters play contextual animations during interactions.
     5→ *
     6→ * Tests run against the local dev server (http://localhost:3000) because this
     7→ * feature lives in the feature/frg-243-contextual-interaction-animations branch,
     8→ * not yet deployed to Vercel.
     9→ *
    10→ * The page does NOT expose window._gameEngine by default, so we intercept
    11→ * index.html via page.route() and inject the exposure before the script executes.
    12→ *
    13→ * Test layout:
    14→ *   1. _getInteractAnim mapping — book/desk/bookshelf→reading, bed/kotatsu/sofa/tv→sit, others→phone
    15→ *   2. Sprite key during interaction — startInteraction sets spriteKey + interactAnim correctly
    16→ *   3. Animation clears after timer — interactAnim null + spriteKey reverts to idle after 2000ms
    17→ *   4. Live sim regression — natural interaction uses a contextual spriteKey (_reading|_sit|_phone)
    18→ *   5. All 9 interaction sprites load without network errors
    19→ */
    20→const { test, expect } = require('@playwright/test');
    21→
    22→const LOCAL_URL = 'http://localhost:3000';
    23→
    24→// ── helpers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    25→
    26→/**
    27→ * Intercept index.html served at / and inject:
    28→ *   window._gameEngine = engine
    29→ *   window.SimulationEngine = SimulationEngine
    30→ * so tests can access the engine and its static methods via page.evaluate().
    31→ */
    32→async function injectEngineExposure(page) {
    33→  await page.route('**/', async (route) => {
    34→    const response = await route.fetch();
    35→    let body = await response.text();
    36→    body = body.replace(
    37→      '// ── Initial state ──',
    38→      '// ── Initial state (QA injection) ──
window._gameEngine = engine;
window.SimulationEngine = SimulationEngine;'
    39→    );
    40→    await route.fulfill({ response, body });
    41→  });
    42→}
    43→
    44→/**
    45→ * Click the start overlay (if visible) and optionally boost time scale.
    46→ */
    47→async function startEngine(page, timeScale = 20) {
    48→  const overlay = page.locator('#start-overlay');
    49→  if (await overlay.isVisible()) {
    50→    await overlay.click();
    51→  }
    52→  await page.evaluate((scale) => {
    53→    if (window._gameEngine) window._gameEngine.timeScale = scale;
    54→  }, timeScale);
    55→}
    56→
    57→/**
    58→ * Poll character states until predicate returns truthy or timeout.
    59→ * Returns the last character state array that satisfied the predicate.
    60→ */
    61→async function pollCharState(page, predicate, timeoutMs = 45_000, intervalMs = 300) {
    62→  const deadline = Date.now() + timeoutMs;
    63→  while (Date.now() < deadline) {
    64→    const chars = await page.evaluate(() =>
    65→      window._gameEngine.characters.map(c => ({
    66→        id: c.id,
    67→        action: c.action,
    68→        interactAnim: c.interactAnim,
    69→        spriteKey: c.spriteKey,
    70→        targetObjectId: c.targetObjectId,
    71→      }))
    72→    );
    73→    if (predicate(chars)) return chars;
    74→    await page.waitForTimeout(intervalMs);
    75→  }
    76→  throw new Error(`pollCharState timed out after ${timeoutMs}ms`);
    77→}
    78→
    79→// ── Tests ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    80→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

    "content": "     1\u2192'use strict';\n     2\u2192/**\n     3\u2192 * REGRESSION \u2014 FRG-243\n     4\u2192 * Characters play contextual animations during interactions.\n     5\u2192 *\n     6\u2192 * Tests run against the local dev server (http://localhost:3000) because this\n     7\u2192 * feature lives in the feature/frg-243-contextual-interaction-animations branch,\n     8\u2192 * not yet deployed to Vercel.\n     9\u2192 *\n    10\u2192 * The page does NOT expose window._gameEngine by default, so we intercept\n    11\u2192 * index.html via page.route() and inject the exposure before the script executes.\n    12\u2192 *\n    13\u2192 * Test layout:\n    14\u2192 *   1. _getInteractAnim mapping \u2014 book/desk/bookshelf\u2192reading, bed/kotatsu/sofa/tv\u2192sit, others\u2192phone\n    15\u2192 *   2. time scale.\n    46\u2192 */\n    47\u2192async function startEngine(page, timeScale = 20) {\n    48\u2192  const overlay = page.locator('#start-overlay');\n    49\u2192  if (await ASSISTANTMESSAGE #94
I see that the older tests set `timeScale` but in the current FRG-245 branch, the `Engine.update()` is called from the Phaser game loop, not the engine itself. The engine doesn't have a `timeScale`. For test 1 (no tile sharing), I can just let the Phaser game loop run naturally and poll at real-time intervals. At real-time, the characters move at `2 tiles/sec` by default. For the live simulation tests, I can just let the natural Phaser loop run and poll character states periodically. For tests that need to programmatically control time (like test 3 - deadlock escape, test 4 - `_buildDynamicWalkable`), I can just call methods directly on the engine without needing to simulate time. Now I'm realizing the core issue: test 1 needs to run a 60-second simulation, but doing that in real time would be impractical. I could manually call `engine.update(1000)` to advance by 1 second at a time, but that conflicts with the Phaser loop also calling update. I need to either pause Phaser and manually step through time, or find another way to control the simulation speed. For the unit tests, I can safely call `engine.update(dt)` multiple times directly within `page.evaluate()` since the Phaser loop is blocked while JavaScript executes synchronously in the browser context. I'm also noting some API changes I need to account for, like renaming `engine.objectEngine.get(id)` to `engine.objectEngine.getById(id)`. tatsu` or `stove` (valid object IDs) 3. `engine._moveCharWithCollision(char, tx, ty)` → `char.moveTo(tx, ty, engine._buildDynamicWalkable(char))` 4. `engine._findFreeInteractTile(objPos, char)` → `engine._findAdjacentWalkable(obj.tx, obj.ty, dynWalkable)` 5. `char._retryCount`, `char._retryTimer`, `char._pendingTarget` → `char._failedMoveCount`, `char.wanderTimer` 6. `engine.manualTick(1)` → `engine.update(5000)` 7. `engine.aiMode = false` → `engine.aiEngine = null` 8. `engine.snapshot()` → direct property access 9. No injection needed since `window._gameEngine` is already exposed in the scene's create function, which runs before the waitForFunction check. The grid is 19 columns by 13 rows, so MAP_COLS needs to be 19 instead of the incorrect 16 from the old test. I should verify that the comment marker used for injection doesn't exist in this codebase version.

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #99
Good. No `// ── Initial state ──` comment and no `start-overlay` element. The `injectEngineExposure` function in the existing test would fail silently (the replace would not find the string) but since `window._gameEngine` is already exposed in scene.js, it still works. The `startEngine` function tries to click `#start-overlay` (which doesn't exist, so `overlay.isVisible()` would return false and skip the click) and then sets `timeScale` (which doesn't exist, so it would just silently fail). So the injection and startEngine functions are harmless but useless. Let me spin up the dev server and run the tests to see what actually breaks, then I can fix the tests based on the real failures.
▶ VIEW RAW DATA

# AI Aquarium — Phaser POC

Proof of concept: same lofi café scene as ai-aquarium, built with Phaser 3 instead of vanilla canvas.

**Purpose:** Compare visual quality and developer effort against the vanilla approach.

**Vanilla version:** https://ai-aquarium-bice.vercel.app

The project is just a static site. To serve it locally, I can use a simple http server like `python3 -m http.server` or `npx serve` or `npx http-server`. The test file uses port 5175. Let me run it with `npx http-server` or `python3 -m http.server 5175` in the background. Actually, looking at the dev server - the task says "run `npm install && npm run dev`" but there's no package.json or dev script. This likely means there's a simple dev server script. Let me check if there's a way to serve this. Since the source imports are ES modules from relative paths (`./src/scene.js?v=15`), it needs an HTTP server (not file:// protocol). I'll start a Python HTTP server on port 5175 to serve the static files.

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #124
As expected, tests 2-7 fail because: 1. `_moveCharWithCollision` doesn't exist → should use `char.moveTo(tx, ty, dynWalkable)` where dynWalkable is built with `_buildDynamicWalkable` 2. `_retryTimer`, `_retryCount` → don't exist 3. `_findFreeInteractTile` → doesn't exist 4. `manualTick` → doesn't exist 5. `engine.objectEngine.get()` → should be `getById()` 6. `cafe_espresso` → not a valid object ID Test 1 actually passes, which confirms the basic collision avoidance is working. Now I need to rewrite the test spec to match the actual API—I'll keep test 1 as-is since it's already passing, but I need to understand why it passes first. The `injectEngineExposure` function searches for a marker in the HTML response and injects the exposure there, so I should trace through how that's working. Looking at the timing issue: `startEngine` tries to set `timeScale` which doesn't actually exist, so the test runs at 1x speed instead of 20x. That means 30 polls at 300ms intervals is only about 9 real seconds, not the 60 simulated seconds the comment claims. Yet the test still passes, which suggests characters either never share tiles in the first place, or the test isn't actually validating what it thinks it is. For the second test, I need to verify that `_moveCharWithCollision` properly uses the dynamic walkable grid to avoid occupied tiles. The implementation builds a grid where other characters' tiles are blocked, then paths around them—but the current `moveTo` doesn't have goal exemption, it just uses the walkable grid as-is. Looking at the pathfinding logic, if the goal tile is marked as blocked in the dynamic grid, the path will return null since the function checks that both the start and goal positions are walkable. So if I try to path to an occupied tile, the pathfinding fails entirely. This means there's no special exemption for the goal tile in the current code. When character A tries to path to character B's exact position, it fails because that tile is blocked. The talk scenario works because character A actually paths to an adjacent tile first using `_findAdjacentWalkable`, not to B's tile directly. So the existing test about goal exemptions is inaccurate and needs to be removed or replaced with a test that reflects what the implementation actually does. For the deadlock escape mechanism, I need to verify how `_failedMoveCount` works: it increments each time `moveTo` fails in `_doWander`, and when it hits 3, the character resets the count and attempts to escape to an adjacent walkable tile. To test this properly, I'd block all paths except one adjacent tile, call `_doWander` twice to build up the failure count, then verify the escape happens on the third call. The challenge is that forcing a `moveTo` failure is difficult—the dynamic walkable grid regenerates each call with many available tiles, so I'd need to surround the character with walls and other characters to guarantee all 15 random attempts fail. Instead, I'll take a simpler approach: directly test `_buildDynamicWalkable` by verifying that other characters' tiles are properly blocked while the static walkable remains unchanged. For deadlock escape, we can test it indirectly: - Set `char._failedMoveCount = 2` - Surround the character with other characters so all paths fail - Call `engine._doWander(char)` - Verify char escapes or `_failedMoveCount` is reset For this to work reliably, I need to ensure the character is in a position where: 1. All 15 random wander attempts fail (because blocked by other chars) 2. At least one adjacent tile is walkable (for escape) Looking at the map: grid is 19×13. Let me find a position near the center where there are walkable tiles in all 4 adjacent directions. Then I can place all other characters at positions that block most paths. Actually, there are only 3 characters total. So we can place 2 characters strategically around the 3rd. With just 2 other characters blocking only 2 tiles, it's hard to make all 15 random attempts fail unless the test character is positioned where every walkable path goes through those 2 blocked tiles. That's getting complicated. Let me pivot to testing `_buildDynamicWalkable` instead—I can verify it correctly marks other characters as blocked by checking that their tiles show as unwalkable in the dynamic walkable map. I need to inspect the actual tilemap to set up these tests properly. For the deadlock escape test, I think the simpler approach is better—just verify that `_failedMoveCount` increments when a character can't move and resets when movement succeeds, which I can test by placing a character in a walled-off area. Looking at the math though, with a 19×13 grid and only 2 tiles blocked by other characters, the odds of all 15 random movement attempts hitting those same blocked tiles is essentially impossible. The only realistic way to trigger the deadlock escape is to create a room with just 2 walkable tiles total—one for the character itself and one other tile. and one neighbor), and use the other 2 chars to block the neighbor. Then: - char at tile A, only walkable neighbor is B - char2 at tile B (blocked) - char3 somewhere else With `dynWalkable`, tile B is blocked. `_getWalkableTiles()` still returns tile B as walkable (it's the STATIC walkable), so the random attempt will try to path to tile B (or other walkable tiles). But `moveTo(B.tx, B.ty, dynWalkable)` would fail because: - `findPath` checks `walkable[goalY][goalX]` - this is `dynWalkable[B.ty][B.tx]` which is `false` → returns null → fails So the character needs to be trapped in a dead-end where all reachable walkable tiles are blocked by other characters. That's too map-specific though. Let me try a simpler approach instead: directly set the failed move counter to 2, stub out `moveTo` to always fail, call the wander function, and verify the counter resets when escape is attempted. Though if the escape tile itself is also blocked, that creates a problem. Looking at the actual code, when moves fail repeatedly, it tries to find an adjacent walkable tile to escape to, but I need to see how that logic handles the case where even the escape tile is unreachable. Setting up a test where I mock `moveTo` to always return false, set the failed move count to 2, then call `_doWander` to verify the count resets to 0 after the escape attempt. For the stability test, I need to use `engine.update()` with a larger time delta instead of manual ticks, since the wander timer decrements happen during character updates within that method. I need to verify that large time deltas work correctly with the movement system—when calling `engine.update()` with 5000ms, a character moving at 2 tiles/sec would traverse 10 tiles in a single frame, but the while loop in the movement logic handles this by processing multiple tile transitions. For the stability test, I'll disable the AI engine and repeatedly call `engine.update()` with large deltas to ensure the simulation advances without breaking. Since JavaScript is single-threaded and `page.evaluate()` blocks the animation loop, running 24 synchronous update calls (totaling 120 simulated seconds) should work fine without interference from Phaser's game loop. Now I need to write out the actual tests, but first I should check whether characters even have an `id` field—I don't see one defined in the Character class.
▶ VIEW RAW DATA

79:    ['Amelia', 'Lucy', 'Ash'].forEach(name => {
80:      this.load.spritesheet(`${name}_idle`, `assets/characters/${name}_idle.png`, {
83:      this.load.spritesheet(`${name}_walk`, `assets/characters/${name}_walk.png`, {
87:        this.load.spritesheet(`${name}_${anim}`, `assets/characters/${name}_${anim}.png`, {
111:    ['Amelia', 'Lucy', 'Ash'].forEach(name => {
113:        key: `${name}_idle_anim`,
114:        frames: this.anims.generateFrameNumbers(`${name}_idle`, { start: 0, end: 5 }),

157:      const sprite = this.add.sprite(initPx, initPy, `${name}_idle`)
160:      sprite.play(`${name}_idle_anim`);
162:      const nameLabel = this.add.text(initPx, initPy - 100, label, {
171:        name, label, tx, ty, sprite, nameLabel,
199:    for (const char of this.engine.characters) {
214:        const label = this.add.text(px, py, ev.emoji, { fontSize: '24px' }).setDepth(25);

217:          y:       label.y - 40,
220:          onComplete: () => label.destroy(),

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #129
So characters don't have an `id` field - they have `name` (Amelia/Lucy/Ash) and `label` (Maple/Sol/Nyx). The test uses `c.id` which would be `undefined`. This is why the test prints `id: 'undefined'`. Let me look at how characters are initialized in scene.js to understand all their properties. Now I have everything I need to rewrite the test. Let me plan: **Characters**: - characters[0]: name='Amelia', label='Maple', tx=6, ty=7 - characters[1]: name='Lucy', label='Sol', tx=10, ty=6 - characters[2]: name='Ash', label='Nyx', tx=13, ty=5 **Grid**: 19 cols × 13 rows **Object IDs**: stove(3,3), fridge(2,3), tea_set(5,5), kotatsu(6,6), tv(9,3), bookshelf(15,3), desk(14,5), bed(16,5), easel(2,7), bonsai(11,8) **Key API corrections**: 1. No `_moveCharWithCollision` → use `char.moveTo(tx, ty, dynWalkable)` 2. No `_findFreeInteractTile` → use `engine._findAdjacentWalkable(obj.tx, obj.ty, dynWalkable)` 3. No `_retryTimer`/`_retryCount`/`_pendingTarget` → use `char._failedMoveCount`, `char.wanderTimer` 4. No `manualTick` → use `engine.update(deltaMs)` 5. No `aiMode` → use `engine.aiEngine = null` 6. No `snapshot()` → direct property access 7. `objectEngine.get(id)` → `objectEngine.getById(id)` 8. MAP_COLS = 19 (not 16) Now I'm going through the test suite and rewriting them with the correct API calls, starting with the tile-sharing test which already works. Writing test for dynamic walkable... Finishing the test assertions, then moving on to the next test case where I need to set up characters at specific positions—maple at (5, 7) heading toward (10, 7), with sol blocking the direct path at (7, 7)—so I can verify the pathfinding uses the dynamic walkable grid to find an alternate route.

     1→import { Character }         from './Character.js';
     2→import { SimulationEngine } from './Engine.js';
     3→import { AIEngine }          from './AIEngine.js';
     4→import { showSpeech }        from './speechBubble.js';
     5→import { OBJECT_DEFS }       from './objects.js';
     6→
     7→// ─── Constants ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
     8→
     9→const TILE_SIZE  = 48;
    10→const GRID_COLS  = 19;
    11→const GRID_ROWS  = 13;
    12→
    13→// Walk spritesheet layout (2304×864, 48 cols × 9 rows, frame size 48×96)
    14→// Frame number = row * 48 + col.  Verified by visual inspection:
    15→//   Row 0 → up   (4 frames:  0–3)
    16→//   Row 3 → right (6 frames: 144–149)
    17→//   Row 6 → down  (6 frames: 288–293)
    18→// LEFT reuses the RIGHT frames with flipX applied at runtime.
    19→const WALK_DIRS = [
    20→  { key: 'up',    start:   0, end:   3 },
    21→  { key: 'down',  start: 288, end: 293 },
    22→  { key: 'right', start: 144, end: 149 },
    23→  { key: 'left',  start: 144, end: 149 }, // mirrored via flipX
    24→];
    25→
    26→// Character definitions — tile positions within walkable area (col 1–17, row 2–9)
    27→const CHARS = [
    28→  {
    29→    name: 'Amelia', label: 'Maple', tx: 6, ty: 7,
    30→    dialogue: ['Perfect brew today~', 'Want some tea?', 'The kitchen smells amazing'],
    31→  },
    32→  {
    33→    name: 'Lucy', label: 'Sol', tx: 10, ty: 6,
    34→    dialogue: ['The bonsai looks healthy', 'I should water the plants', 'Such peaceful light...'],
    35→  },
    36→  {
    37→    name: 'Ash', label: 'Nyx', tx: 13, ty: 5,
    38→    dialogue: ['I need more paint...', 'This light is perfect for sketching', 'Where did I put my brush?'],
    39→  },
    40→];
    41→
    42→// ─── Walkable grid ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    43→// 19 cols × 13 rows.
    44→// Interior floor (main room): cols 1–17, rows 2–9.
    45→// Rows 0–1 = roof/top wall; rows 10–12 = genkan / exterior — not walkable.
    46→
    47→function buildWalkableGrid() {
    48→  const grid = [];
    49→  for (let row = 0; row < GRID_ROWS; row++) {
    50→    const r = [];
    51→    for (let col = 0; col < GRID_COLS; col++) {
    52→      r.push(col >= 1 && col <= 17 && row >= 2 && row <= 9);
    53→    }
    54→    grid.push(r);
    55→  }
    56→  // Mark tiles occupied by objects as non-walkable
    57→  for (const def of OBJECT_DEFS) {
    58→    const { tx, ty } = def.position;
    59→    if (ty >= 0 && ty < GRID_ROWS && tx >= 0 && tx < GRID_COLS) {
    60→      grid[ty][tx] = false;
    61→    }
    62→  }
    63→  return grid;
    64→}
    65→
    66→// ─── Scene ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    67→
    68→export class CafeScene extends Phaser.Scene {
    69→  constructor() { super({ key: 'CafeScene' }); }
    70→
    71→  // ── Preload ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    72→
    73→  preload() {
    74→    this.load.image('jp', 'assets/designs/Japanese_Home_1_preview_48x48.png');
    75→
    76→    // Interaction animation types → spritesheets (48×96 frames, 6 frames each)
    77→    const INTERACT_ANIMS = ['reading_48x48', 'sit_48x48', 'sit2_48x48', 'phone_48x48'];
    78→
    79→    ['Amelia', 'Lucy', 'Ash'].forEach(name => {
    80→      this.load.spritesheet(`${name}_idle`, `assets/characters/${name}_idle.png`, {

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #132
Now I understand the walkable grid: - Interior floor: cols 1–17, rows 2–9 (16 cols × 8 rows = 128 tiles) - Object tiles are marked non-walkable Object positions (non-walkable): - stove: (3, 3) - fridge: (2, 3) - tea_set: (5, 5) - kotatsu: (6, 6) - tv: (9, 3) - bookshelf: (15, 3) - desk: (14, 5) - bed: (16, 5) - easel: (2, 7) - bonsai: (11, 8) Walkable tiles are in cols 1-17, rows 2-9, minus the above objects. For the pathfinding test, I'm placing maple at (1, 7) and sol as a blocker at (4, 7) with the goal at (8, 7)—the pathfinding needs to route around the blocked tile, potentially using row 6 as an alternate path. I need to be more systematic about which tiles are actually walkable. Row 7 has the easel blocking (2,7), so I can move through columns 1, 3-17 there. Row 8 is mostly clear except for the bonsai at (11,8). Let me simplify the test case: I'll place the character at (1,5) and use a blocker at (5,5) where the tea is. Wait, that won't work since (5,5) is an object tile. Let me reconsider the layout instead. I'll put the character at (1,6) with the goal at (8,6), and place a blocker at (4,6) to force a detour. Row 6 is walkable across columns 1-17 except for the kotatsu at (6,6), so (4,6) is definitely passable. This setup means the direct path gets blocked at (4,6), forcing the pathfinding to find an alternate route around the obstacle. I'm realizing this is getting too tangled with all the object placements. Let me simplify: I'll put the maple at (1,4) with the goal at (8,4), place a blocker at (4,4), and think through the pathfinding more systematically—the direct route is blocked, so the algorithm would need to route around via row 3 or row 5 instead. Row 3 walkable: cols 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 16, 17 (all others minus objects at 2, 3, 9, 15). A route from (1,4) to (8,4) with (4,4) blocked: - Try going via row 3: (1,4)→(1,3)→(1,3)... wait row 3, col 1 is walkable. - (1,4)→(1,3)→(2,3)? No, fridge at (2,3) blocks it. So (1,3)→(1,4)... that's a loop. - Try going via row 5: (1,4)→(1,5)→(2,5)→(3,5)→(4,5)→(5,5)? No, tea_set at (5,5). - (4,5)→(4,4)? That's the blocked tile. - Hmm. (4,5)→(5,4) if we go right from (4,5)→(5,5) BLOCKED, or (4,5)→(4,4) BLOCKED... Let me try a different approach with more open space. Actually wait, let me reconsider. The alternative path when (4,4) is blocked: - From (3,4) the A* can't go to (4,4), it goes to (3,3) or (3,5) - (3,3): row 3, col 3 = stove! NOT walkable. - (3,5) is walkable, so that's the way forward. From (3,5) I can reach (4,5), which is walkable. But (5,5) has the tea_set blocking it, and (4,4) is blocked too. Going right to (4,6) is walkable but that creates a long detour to reach (8,4). The pathfinding might fail entirely if these blockages cut off all routes, or it'll have to take a significantly longer path around the obstacles. Going right along row 4, (4,4) is blocked. We need to go around. Options: go up to row 3 (but blocked by stove/fridge) or down to row 5. Row 5 has tea_set at (5,5). So: - (1,4)→(2,4)→(3,4)→(3,5)→(4,5)→(4,4)? Wait, (4,4) is blocked in dynWalkable. - (4,5)→(5,5)? tea_set, blocked in STATIC walkable. - So from (4,5) the only options are: (4,4) BLOCKED, (5,5) BLOCKED, (4,6), (3,5). - (4,6) leads into row 6... kotatsu at (6,6) but (4,6) is fine. - (4,5)→(4,6)→(5,6)→(6,6)? kotatsu, BLOCKED. - (5,6)→(5,5)? BLOCKED. (5,6)→(5,4)? (5,4) is walkable! - Path: ...(4,5)→(4,6)→(5,6)→(5,4)? That's not right - A* is 4-directional. Wait, A* uses cardinal neighbors only. So from (5,6): - neighbors: (4,6), (6,6) [kotatsu BLOCKED], (5,5) [tea_set BLOCKED], (5,7) - So (5,7) is the only open neighbor, continuing the search from there. Actually, this is getting too tangled. Let me simplify the test case. I'll place maple at (1,2) trying to reach (12,2), with a blocker at (6,2). Row 2 itself should be clear, so the path needs to detour around via row 3, which has obstacles at specific positions like the stove, fridge, and TV. From (1,2) I can move right to (5,2), then up to (5,3) which should be walkable. From there I can move right through (6,3) and (7,3), then back down to (7,2) and continue right to the goal. The blocker at (6,2) shouldn't appear in the final path. For testing, I'll teleport the spawned characters directly to their test positions by setting their coordinates. Now I'm checking whether the pathfinding function will accept these test positions—the start and goal tiles both need to be walkable according to the grid boundaries. For Maple starting at (1,2) and Sol blocking (6,2), I need to verify that the dynamic walkability map correctly marks Sol's position as impassable. I need to verify that `maple.tx` and `maple.ty` are set correctly so the rounding works out, and confirm that `moveTo` uses the `walkable` parameter passed to it rather than the character's own walkable property. Looking at how `moveTo` calls `findPath` with the walkable grid and the character's rounded position as the start point. Now checking the `findPath` function to see how it validates the start and goal positions against the walkable grid. Since maple is at position (1, 2) and is excluded from the dynamic walkable grid, the cell at `dynWalkable[2][1]` should contain the static terrain value, which is true. Similarly, the goal position at `dynWalkable[2][12]` should also be true since no character occupies that cell. So the path should be valid. Now I need to verify that tile (6, 2) doesn't appear in the calculated path and that this is indeed where sol is blocking movement. Setting up a test... Setting up Test 4 to verify the deadlock escape mechanism by positioning maple at a safe tile, pre-setting its failed move counter to 2, and then mocking the moveTo function to force additional failures that should trigger the escape logic. So `failedMoveCountAfter === 0`. Now I'm setting up a stability test that disables the AI engine and runs a 120-second simulation across 24 ticks, tracking character positions and activity states throughout. Running the simulation loop... I'm filtering out characters that never became active during the simulation and returning them as stuck, along with any errors that occurred and the total simulation time. Now I'm reconsidering how the engine update works—with the AI engine disabled, characters won't pursue targets, but their wander timers will still decrement. A character starting with a wander timer around 7000ms will drop below zero after just a couple of ticks at 5000ms per tick, triggering the wander behavior. I'm tracing through the timing logic to verify characters actually wander—the wanderTimer gets decremented each frame, so within a couple ticks it should drop below zero and trigger the wander behavior. There's a potential issue emerging with the movement speed that I need to investigate. Writing movement logic... Still writing animation logic... I think this approach will work since the Phaser game is running in the browser context. Let me write out the full replacement test file. I should also double-check the `injectEngineExposure` and `startEngine` functions—since `window._gameEngine` is already exposed in scene.js, I don't strictly need the injection route, but I can leave it as a harmless no-op. Actually, I should remove that injection route entirely since it intercepts all `/` requests and could interfere with asset loading. Looking at the glob pattern `**/`, it only matches URLs ending with `/`, so the index route matches but asset paths like `/src/scene.js` don't. The injection would just add unnecessary latency without breaking anything, so I'll simplify by dropping it entirely and just navigating to the URL while waiting for the engine to be ready. Now I'm thinking through an edge case in Test 3 where `maple.moveTo(12, 2, dynWalkable)` is called, and I need to verify this works correctly with the current setup. I need to be careful about the Phaser game loop running between my evaluate calls—steps in `maple.path` might get consumed before I can inspect them. The safest approach is to do everything in a single `page.evaluate()` call: set positions, build the walkable map, call `moveTo`, and capture the path immediately, all synchronously without the rAF loop interrupting. Since JavaScript is single-threaded, once I'm inside an evaluate callback, nothing else runs until I return. For the stability test, running `engine.update(5000)` twenty-four times processes 120 seconds of simulation instantly, which advances character positions and updates sprites accordingly—this should be safe since the sprite coordinates get synced in each update call. The main thing to verify is that `objectEngine.tick()` with large deltaMs values doesn't cause unexpected behavior. With a 5-second delta, state timers on objects could trigger multiple transitions if they're not handled correctly. I need to check whether objectEngine properly manages these auto-transitions so they don't fire repeatedly within a single large tick.

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #135
The object ticking looks straightforward - it just decrements the auto timer and fires transitions. With deltaMs=5000, it might fire multiple state transitions in one tick, but that should be OK since it only fires once per tick call. Actually looking at lines 343-352: ```js tick(deltaMs) { ... if (!s?.auto_next) return null; this.state = s.auto_next.state; ``` It seems like it just sets the state once per tick, not looping. So even with large deltaMs, it fires at most once per tick call. That's fine for our purposes. Now I'm ready to rewrite the collision-avoidance test file. I need to strip out the unnecessary setup like `injectEngineExposure` and `startEngine`, then swap the API calls to use the actual methods available - replacing `_moveCharWithCollision` with `char.moveTo()` and `_findFreeInteractTile` with `_findAdjacentWalkable`. I'll also need to update the test data references like swapping `cafe_espresso` for `kotatsu`, fixing the character identifier to use `c.label` instead of `c.id`, and adjusting how I simulate time progression with `engine.update()` instead of `manualTick()`. The core test structure stays the same - five test areas covering tile sharing, movement validation, and interaction handling - but everything runs against the actual API surface. Writing the test file... mostly as-is, fix `c.id` → `c.label`) 2. `_buildDynamicWalkable` marks other chars as blocked, preserves own tile, doesn't modify static grid 3. `moveTo` uses dynamic walkable to route around a blocking character's tile 4. Deadlock escape: `_failedMoveCount` resets to 0 after third wander failure and escape fires 5. Live sim stability: no crashes and all chars move within 120s of engine.update() calls For test 1, the issue is that `startEngine(page, 20)` doesn't actually speed up the Phaser loop—it just runs at normal speed. So I need to either let the game run in real-time and poll positions over many seconds, or programmatically advance the simulation with `engine.update(deltaMs)` calls. The tricky part is avoiding double-ticking if Phaser is already running its own loop. The cleanest approach is probably to just rely on the real-time Phaser loop and check positions periodically without manually advancing sim time. enough to validate collision avoidance. The misleading comment about 20x speed needs to go, but the test itself works fine. Let me write cleaner tests: keep test 1 mostly as-is since it already passes, add a synchronous check for _buildDynamicWalkable, another for moveTo routing around obstacles, and a fourth to verify the deadlock escape mechanism resets the failed move counter. Writing test implementations...

  "content": {
    "type": "thinking",
    "thinking": "The object ticking looks straightforward - it just decrements the auto timer and fires transitions. With deltaMs=5000, it might fire multiple state transitions in one tick, but that should be OK since it only fires once per tick call. Actually looking at lines 343-352:\n\n```js\ntick(deltaMs) {\n  ...  \n  if (!s?.auto_next) return null;\n  this.state = s.auto_next.state;\n```\n\nIt seems like it just sets the state once per tick, not looping. So even with large deltaMs, it fires at most once per tick call. That's fine for our purposes.\n\n\nNow I'm ready to rewrite the collision-avoidance test file. I need to strip out the unnecessary setup like `injectEngineExposure` and `startEngine`, then swap the API calls to use the actual methods available - replacing `_moveCharWithCollision` with `char.moveTo()` and `_findFreeInteractTile` with `_findAdjacentWalkable`.\n\nI'll also need to update the test data references like swapping `cafe_espresso` for `kotatsu`, fixing the character identifier to use `c.label` instead of `c.id`, and adjusting how I simulate time progression with `engine.update()` instead of `manualTick()`. The core test structure stays the same - five test areas covering tile sharing, movement validation, and interaction handling - but everything runs against the actual API surface.\n\nWriting the test file... mostly as-is, fix `c.id` \u2192 `c.label`)\n2. `_buildDynamicWalkable` marks other chars as blocked, preserves own tile, doesn't modify static grid\n3. `moveTo` uses dynamic walkable to route around a blocking character's tile\n4. Deadlock escape: `_failedMoveCount` resets to 0 after third wander failure and escape fires\n5. Live sim stability: no crashes and all chars move within 120s of engine.update() calls\n\nFor test 1, the issue is that `startEngine(page, 20)` doesn't actually speed up the Phaser loop\u2014it just runs at normal speed. So I need to either let the game run in real-time and poll positions over many seconds, or programmatically advance the simulation with `engine.update(deltaMs)` calls. The tricky part is avoiding double-ticking if Phaser is already running its own loop. The cleanest approach is probably to just rely on the real-time Phaser loop and check positions periodically without manually advancing sim time. enough to validate collision avoidance. The misleading comment about 20x speed needs to go, but the test itself works fine. Let me write cleaner tests: keep test 1 mostly as-is since it already passes, add a synchronous check for _buildDynamicWalkable, another for moveTo routing around obstacles, and a fourth to verify the deadlock escape mechanism resets the failed move counter.\n\nWriting test implementations...",
    "signature":
